content.problemStatement:
Many internet protocols these days include the option of associating a
media type with the content being sent.
The type is usually inferred from the file extension.
You are to write a program that facilitates the lookup of media types for
a number of files.

You will be given a table of media type associations that associate a certain
file extension with a certain media type.
You will then be given a number of file names, and tasked to determine the correct
media type for each file.
A file extension is defined as the part of the file name after the final period.
If a file name has no periods, then it has no extension and the media type cannot
be determined.
If the file extension is not present in the table, then the media type cannot be
determined.
In such cases you will print "unknown" as the media type.
If the file extension does appear in the table (case matters), then print the associated
media type.
constraints: File extensions consist only of letters and digits, length <= 10, and are distinct within a test case.
Media types consist of printable characters without spaces, length <= 50.
File names consist of letters, digits and periods, length <= 50.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. For each test case: a line with two integers N and Q, where N is the number of known file-extension to media-type associations and Q is the number of file names to query. N following lines each contain a file extension and its media type, separated by a single space. Q following lines each contain a file name.
content.outputFormat: For each of the Q file names of each test case, output on its own line the associated media type. If the file has no extension, the extension is not present in the table, or the lookup is case-sensitive and fails, output 'unknown'.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
5 6
html text/html
htm text/html
png image/png
svg image/svg+xml
txt text/plain
index.html
this.file.has.lots.of.dots.txt
nodotsatall
virus.exe
dont.let.the.png.fool.you
case.matters.TXT
sampleTestCases.output:
text/html
text/plain
unknown
unknown
unknown
unknown
sampleTestCases.explanation: The table maps 'html' and 'txt' to their types. Extensions are extracted after the last period. Case matters, so 'TXT' does not match 'txt'.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
2 2
jpg image/jpeg
png image/png
image.jpg
image.png
sampleTestCases.explanation: The table maps 'jpg' and 'png' to their types. Extensions are extracted after the last period.
The file has no extension, so the media type cannot be determined.

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.input:
1
100 100
html text/html
htm text/html
png image/png
svg image/svg+xml
txt text/plain
index.html
this.file.has.lots.of.dots.txt
nodotsatall
virus.exe
dont.let.the.png.fool.you
case.matters.TXT
hidden.expectedOutput:
text/html
text/plain
unknown
unknown
unknown
unknown

hidden.label: stress/general
hidden.input:
2
2 2
jpg image/jpeg
png image/png
image.jpg
image.png
2 2
html text/html
htm text/html
index.html
this.file.has.lots.of.dots.htm

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by using a hash table to store the file extensions and their corresponding media types. For each file name, we extract the file extension and look it up in the hash table. If the extension is found, we print the corresponding media type; otherwise, we print 'unknown'.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N + Q)) per test case, O(T * (N + Q)) total. The hash table operations (insertion and lookup) take constant time on average.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the space used by the hash table to store the file extensions and their corresponding media types.
